metadata.description: WHY-HOW educational pairs matching ROOT_CAUSE quotes to SOLUTION/MECHANISM quotes for SpeedTrac marketing

pairs.why_quote: Overspeed training is, exactly as it sounds, the practice of training your limbs to turnover at a higher speed - a speed over what feels comfortable or natural. Through overspeed training, not only does your brain literally learn how to fire faster and control your muscles more efficiently at higher speeds, but you also develop more powerful and quick muscle fiber contractions.
pairs.how_quote: I went from a driver swing speed of 104 up to 115 doing a year of SuperSpeed. If I had to start it all over again though I would definitely go with The Stack due to the app.

pairs.why_quote: The combination of Overload Training (heavy) and Underload Training (light) is the fastest known way to increase clubhead speed. Overload strengthens the specific muscles of the swing. Underload trains your nervous system to fire faster.
pairs.how_quote: After about 2 months my regular driver ss went from 105 to 112-113. Most of the initial gains with all of these systems is your brain figuring out what fast feels like.

pairs.why_quote: Speed training requires only brief doses of low volume, high-output work. This means each session is very short, but very fast. It is not meant to be metabolic conditioning. If you're exhausting yourself metabolically, it becomes very difficult to train your nervous system.
pairs.how_quote: I've had success just swinging my driver 10-20 times as fast as possible a few times a week. Similar to what I've seen a stack session would look like but just with my driver on my launch monitor tracking club head speed.

pairs.why_quote: The science behind Complex Training is that the strength set 'primes' the central nervous system so that more muscle fibers are available for the subsequent explosive exercise.
pairs.how_quote: For every MPH of added clubhead speed, you'll gain 3 yards of extra distance. By working the Rypstick into your training routine for just 10 minutes a day, 3x per week, you could see 15-25 yards added to your driving distance.

pairs.why_quote: Clubhead speed is the single greatest statistical correlate to a golfer's handicap. If you want to get better, you must get faster.
pairs.how_quote: I haven't come across anything that I would recommend to a friend until I tried Super Speed Training... I highly recommend it. It works.

pairs.why_quote: Every mile per hour of driver speed is worth roughly 2.5 yards of distance. A golfer with a 90 mph driver speed has an average handicap of ~14. A golfer with a 100 mph driver speed has an average handicap of ~5.
pairs.how_quote: Ive recommended Superspeed to lots of people, no one has taken me up on it. Despite the fact that they often comment on how much farther I'm hitting it.

pairs.why_quote: An OPEN face relative to the PATH is the ONE and ONLY real REASON you hit a slice.
pairs.how_quote: I'm a 72 year old with a 16 handicap. I have used the Blue Brick for just two 1 hr practice sessions and have turned my drives from 200 yd weak fades to 240 yd slight draws EVERY single time!!! This device has helped me 'feel' what it means to be on plane and properly shallow the club.

pairs.why_quote: A slice happens when the clubface is open at impact relative to the swing path. While that might seem simple, there are underlying factors that cause the clubface to open in the first place.
pairs.how_quote: If had to give this brick a title, it would be 'I learned how to hit draws in one range session.' This brick is incredible and I would highly recommend this to any golfer of any level.

pairs.why_quote: Most players KNOW that. And while I could break down the old and new ball-flight laws that prove it... The bottom line is... All slice-fix training, draw-biased clubs, and virtually every training aid to fix a slice... All 'attempt' to do the SAME THING for you... Square your clubface relative to the path at impact.
pairs.how_quote: The only one I would give the nod to, is the momentus power hittier. The weighted clubs help you ingrain a good swing. If your timing is off, you'll be off balance, but if it's right, you have a nice smooth finish.

pairs.why_quote: A weak grip can lead to an open clubface at impact, making it difficult to control the ball's flight.
pairs.how_quote: have tried everything except the grip, which I believed I had OK as the irons went straight and a reasonable distance. Then I had an eureka moment when I tried to grip much more in the fingers. Low and behold the drives went as straight as a die.

pairs.why_quote: Keeping your weight on your back foot reduces your ability to square the clubface.
pairs.how_quote: I also use the Sklz Gold Flex as a warm up, golf swing trainer, and for exercise. In addition I would recommend a Strike Right Golf Swing Trainer. If you want to train an under, out and up swing path, the Strike Right will help do that as it also helps fix the over the top move.

pairs.why_quote: Training in the gym. This method will show the least increase in speed. Gym training is really for enabling your body to handle 2 and 3 above and for preparing the body to be able to output more force.
pairs.how_quote: Since surgery, I put my efforts into working on my swing mechanics to make it easier on my body, and in the last year really putting effort into my over all physical fitness. I have seen bigger gains from both of those then I saw from speed training.

pairs.why_quote: 100 balls all out is excessive especially for someone just starting out. Better to start with 20 then increase from there while working on the other stuff in the gym. OP probably would need to increase his endurance/stamina as well.
pairs.how_quote: You should try cross-fitting. I have been playing golf for five years now, and I can easily say that I have always improved my skill, but the only thing I was lacking was the physique. I decided to change that, and now I've been consistently training for seven months, and I've never felt so good. My game improved drastically.

pairs.why_quote: In most cases, when your nervous system is drained from high-intensity efforts or too much stress, it needs 48 hours to fully recover.
pairs.how_quote: Cost no object, The Stack hands down. Enforced rest periods through Grit score. I'm an impatient person and with the SuperSpeed protocols I never gave myself enough rest between sets.

pairs.why_quote: When you train for power, your brain, spinal cord and entire central nervous system learn to control your muscles in a far more efficient way, creating enhanced muscle utilization without the negative effects of too much muscle bulk.
pairs.how_quote: I have the Supersticks and after 6 weeks have seen a 5 mph increase on the course and roughly 10 yards gain with my driver.

pairs.why_quote: I personally was a proponent of Stack. Through using it, I saw fairly significant improvements in my numbers on Stack. I stress that only to say that I found that the crossover of benefits of Stack training to my actual on course speeds hit a plateau very quickly.
pairs.how_quote: I have them both, and I think they do basically the same thing. Several years ago, I gained 14mph of CHS just by hitting balls with drivers of differing weights. The concept is proven. Find the one that gets you hooked to do it consistently over the course of 9 mos (yes 9 mos if you want it to become permanent) and you will hit the ball farther.

pairs.why_quote: I had been using the SSG protocols for almost a year before I went to Par4Success (Chris Finn's facility) and I had seen little if any change on the golf course.
pairs.how_quote: I think it comes down to whether or not you're a guy who swings hard to begin with or if you're a guy who swings for control. If you're the latter, then using your own driver and just going after it, over and over again, yes, you'll gain some swing speed.

pairs.why_quote: Whether or not ANY swing speed training will work depends on the individual and what currently keeps him/her from swinging faster. This is NOT a one size fits all situation. Far, far more golfers are held back by technique and/or physical limitations like hip mobility, than simply by not swinging fast enough.
pairs.how_quote: Doing stack will improve your foundation IMO. My driver accuracy improved as did my speed post stack. The same things that improve your sequence generally add speed.

pairs.why_quote: I was swinging about 110 5 yrs ago and now I am at 99 and it is frustrating. I am 59 and play 1-2 times a week and practice at least 3 times a week. Hoping to get close to the 110 I was or at least 107 to be competitive.
pairs.how_quote: I've been using the System since December and am now 7 weeks into protocol 1. I'm 64 years old, in good shape, and trying to get back some speed lost over the years. I started at 98-100MPH and after 8 weeks my driver swing speed is pretty consistent at 103-105.

pairs.why_quote: Many slices are caused by an over-the-top swing path, which cuts across the ball from outside to inside, leaving the clubface open.
pairs.how_quote: CONDITIONAL: Tour Striker Plane mate - if you are over the top, all arms, or struggle with flipping the club or thins and shanks.

pairs.why_quote: If your body isn't rotating properly during the swing, your arms tend to take over, causing an open clubface and a slice.
pairs.how_quote: I've had good success with the Tour Striker Planemate and Smartball and a golf club for stretching to increase flexibility and ability to turn.

pairs.why_quote: Over-the-top/out-to-in swing path: This happens when the upper body dominates the downswing, causing the club to cut across the ball.
pairs.how_quote: I have the medicus 2000 dual hinge, and for me it was worth the price of admission. I still swing it almost everyday to keep my swing in sync. It helped me with my tempo, and getting the club in the correct position through out my back swing.

pairs.why_quote: I think the biggest problem is overswinging. I think my mentality on the tee is to crush it, 300 yards, which for me leads to my lower body firing too quickly, my grip being overly tight and being behind the ball a bit either blocking it right or trying to flip and catch up to it.
pairs.how_quote: swing weight helped me shorten my swing to parallel cuz if i went to far my swing became unbalanced etc so it made me shorten my swing to parallel to keep a balanced swing.

pairs.why_quote: Dwight Howard's practice free-throw percentage was a stellar 81.7%. But next to it, his performance in real games was 49.5%. This wasn't a minor dip; it was a catastrophic Transfer Gap, a 32.2% chasm between his potential and his performance.
pairs.how_quote: I like that you can actually hit a ball so you still have to have some resemblance of technique when doing this vs just ripping max effort swings at air.

pairs.why_quote: My SS is around 101mph and I brought these to the sim the other night... I was able to get 138mph my fastest to date with the light stick and 122mph with the heavy. Than I teed one up and I got 105mph on trackman. Why is there such a big drop off from swinging the heavy to actually swinging my driver???
pairs.how_quote: Using stack, speed sticks, etc - easily the fastest way to get faster. However, there are caveats...it may make you way worse. Moving a bad swing faster very likely will result in bad shots being more wayward.

pairs.why_quote: The initial neuromuscular changes that Stack drives just from an intent standpoint added a quick ~5 mph of head speed to my driver, that I would say generally came with me to the golf course. However, I never saw much change in iron head speeds, and I could not seem to progress beyond those initial gains when I was actually playing golf.
pairs.how_quote: There is plenty of science to support the concept of overspeed training in a variety of sports. It's out there if you want to find it. It is the most effective way to increase speed quickly. Just swinging your driver (or a broom, for that matter) hard for a couple dozens swings a few times a week will make you faster.

pairs.why_quote: It definitely works. I'm scratch and had 108 chs. I got up to 117 with the training in just a few weeks. However, in my case, I became flippy. I found I couldn't maintain my drive and hold golf swing that was mainly pivot driven. I developed way too much flip/roll release to generate the speed. So I stopped the training bc my accuracy fell off badly.
pairs.how_quote: My advice these days is to fix your swing first and max out your ability that way, then go into speed training if you want more. You can't out train a bad swing.

pairs.why_quote: Used the SuperSpeed and followed the training protocol strictly for about two months. My swing speed definitely increased. A good hit of my driver goes 10-15 yards further. However, the biggest problem is it will break your tempo and rhythm since you will try to swing and hit as hard as possible.
pairs.how_quote: I find that the stack is far easier to use than SuperSpeed. It is more expensive but not really that much more in the end. You need a speed tracking device with both so the cost difference is a what you see, what you get sort of thing.

pairs.why_quote: Swinging harder doesn't mean hitting farther. If anything, it does the opposite.
pairs.how_quote: I have built many things as well as purchased many. The only one I would actually recommend would be the Orange Whip. Similar to the Sklz Gold Flex. These are something you can always use no matter what your ability is. Meaning you won't grow out of it.

pairs.why_quote: Overswinging is another frequent mistake, as golfers often try to generate maximum distance through effort rather than technique. Focus on making a controlled, balanced swing at 80-85% effort often produces better results than swinging as hard as possible.
pairs.how_quote: This is really the most important aspect to any training aid, commit to using it! if you aren't going to incorporate into your practice routine on a regular basis you are wasting your money. I spend at least 15-minutes 3+ times a week on my practice green.

pairs.why_quote: I had inconsistent grip pressure, tempo and swingspeed that was leading to my erratic driving. And the erratic driving was leading to a loss of confidence and then over correcting.
pairs.how_quote: I shot a personal best 70 this summer and lowered my handicap down to a 1 using the Top Speed Golf system along with the Blue Brick. Can't wait to make even more progress this fall! TSG combined with the Blue Brick is the best lesson/training combo I've ever used.

pairs.why_quote: The body is going to be an auto regulator so if you have swing issues the body isn't going to let you swing faster during a golf swing.
pairs.how_quote: The Rypstick takes the negatives of the SuperSpeed system (multiple sticks) and solves that with the changeable weight design.

pairs.why_quote: you need to sequence properly and use the ground. Once you start doing that you can figure out what the limiting factor is, cause until you sequence right and use the ground those will be the limiting factors.
pairs.how_quote: Ok, lets address the question at hand, its Superspeed vs Stack, i prefer the stack because it comes with a app that helps guide you through exercises and changes the routine and weights due to whatever results you are getting.

pairs.why_quote: A major cause of slicing is an outside-to-inside swing path. To fix this, you need to train your body to swing more from the inside.
pairs.how_quote: Best one I have used is a shoe box... placed outside the ball line maybe 1/4 to 1/2 of an inch from the toe of your club at address. It perfectly lines up with your target line and you immediately know if you are swinging inside-to-square-to-inside.

pairs.why_quote: The Law of Opposites is the fastest way to fix a bias: to cure a slice, you must first master the hook. This is not a 'quick fix'; it is a neurological necessity for recalibrating your internal athletic compass.
pairs.how_quote: Contrary to what most golfers do, the fix is complex. The golfers' tendency is to open up more and pull harder left to get the ball moving left. What this normally does is impart MORE spin so the ball will go harder right.

notes: Pairs demonstrate scientific foundation for overspeed/overload training that SpeedTrac uses
Face control and weight positioning pairs align with Y-Trac Weight System benefits
Transfer problem pairs highlight SpeedTrac's advantage of being swing-specific with real clubhead
Speed-accuracy pairs support SpeedTrac's combined approach vs single-dimension speed sticks
